name: mat
shortDescription: A plain woven mat for floors, doorways, stalls, or sleeping corners where bare boards feel too cold and stone feels too honest. It is humble stock, but travelers, monks, stablehands, and poor households all know the worth of a clean place to stand or kneel.
flavorText: “A mat is a small mercy between feet and the world.”

name: rug
shortDescription: A serviceable rug with enough color and thickness to warm a room without pretending to be noble. It sells well to inns, homes, and traders setting up better stalls, because even modest cloth can make a floor look less like a storage problem.
flavorText: “Put a rug down and suddenly the same room asks for manners.”

name: wooden door
shortDescription: A standard wooden door with sturdy planks, iron fittings, and the useful promise of keeping weather, animals, and some people outside. Builders buy it by need, landlords by the dozen, and everyone tests the hinge before trusting the price.
flavorText: “Civilization begins when someone decides a hole needs manners.”

name: fancy extravagant door
shortDescription: An extravagant door carved and dressed to announce wealth before anyone knocks. It is too costly for ordinary houses and too noticeable for secretive owners, but perfect for guild halls, noble estates, and merchants who want guests impressed before being overcharged.
flavorText: “A rich door does half the boasting before its owner speaks.”

name: fancy drawer dresser
shortDescription: A polished dresser with decorative drawers, built for chambers where clothing, papers, and private little vanities must be stored beautifully. The craftsmanship makes it practical, but the finish makes buyers imagine their lives becoming tidier and more expensive.
flavorText: “A fine drawer hides disorder with admirable confidence.”

name: fancy mat
shortDescription: A decorative mat woven with better thread and more pride than a doorway strictly requires. It suits shrines, guest rooms, refined shops, or any threshold where the owner wants visitors to notice care before they notice dust.
flavorText: “Even a doorstep can learn ceremony.”

name: fancy rug
shortDescription: A handsome rug with richer patterning, brighter dyes, and enough softness to make plain floors feel embarrassed. Nobles like it for comfort, merchants like it for display, and appraisers like checking whether the colors are true or merely loud.
flavorText: “Good rugs make guests look down and still feel impressed.”

name: fancy compartment dresser
shortDescription: A refined dresser divided into neat compartments for jewelry, folded cloth, letters, perfumes, or whatever small secrets a household wants arranged. It fetches a premium from organized minds and from disorganized ones hoping furniture will do the thinking for them.
flavorText: “Little compartments make little lies easier to find.”

name: fancy two door dresser
shortDescription: A fine dresser with paired doors and polished frontage, broad enough to hold garments while looking dignified against a chamber wall. It is furniture for buyers who want storage to feel like architecture rather than a pile of drawers.
flavorText: “Two doors, one promise: the mess is someone else's problem now.”

name: dresser
shortDescription: A common dresser of useful size, made for folded clothes, linens, household tools, or a merchant's spare inventory when proper shelves run out. It is neither grand nor shabby, which makes it easy to sell almost anywhere people own more than one shirt.
flavorText: “The dresser is proof that belongings multiply faster in the dark.”

name: round dresser
shortDescription: A round dresser with an unusual shape, more decorative than the square sort and trickier to fit against a sensible wall. Buyers choose it for parlors, guest rooms, and eccentric homes where useful storage must also start conversations.
flavorText: “Corners are efficient. Curves are expensive.”

name: dresser with mirror
shortDescription: A dresser fitted with a mirror, combining storage with the daily ritual of judging one's own presentation. It is popular with nobles, performers, inn suites, and anyone who wants to discover problems before the room does.
flavorText: “A mirror makes furniture twice as honest and half as forgiving.”

name: large white wooden door
shortDescription: A large white wooden door, bright, formal, and difficult to ignore once installed. Temples, townhouses, and wealthy homes favor it for entrances that should look clean and important, though every porter knows white paint remembers muddy hands.
flavorText: “White doors declare purity, then spend years arguing with weather.”

name: iron gate door
shortDescription: An iron gate door with bars, hinges, and a blunt sense of purpose. It lets guards see through while still saying no, making it valuable for courtyards, cellars, guarded gardens, storehouses, and any doorway where privacy matters less than control.
flavorText: “A gate is a door that wants witnesses.”

name: large dark wooden door
shortDescription: A large dark wooden door with heavy color, broad boards, and the sober mood of old halls. It suits keeps, guild houses, warehouses, and inns that want an entrance to feel serious enough that guests lower their voices before entering.
flavorText: “Dark wood has a talent for making thresholds feel older.”

name: large wooden door
shortDescription: A large wooden door built for big frames, busy entrances, and buildings that need more authority than a cottage plank can provide. It takes strong hinges and patient haulers, but once fitted it makes a room feel properly guarded.
flavorText: “A large door does not open; it grants permission.”

name: wooden door with windows
shortDescription: A wooden door set with small window panes, letting light and suspicion pass through in equal measure. Shopkeepers, inns, and townhouses like it because visitors can be seen before being welcomed, refused, or made to wait politely.
flavorText: “Glass in a door turns knocking into negotiation.”

name: solid iron door
shortDescription: A solid iron door made for vaults, prisons, strongrooms, and owners who would rather pay once than worry every night. It is heavy, costly, and unpleasant to move, but buyers trust it because iron keeps poor company outside.
flavorText: “Some doors protect rooms. This one threatens the hallway.”

name: large light wooden door
shortDescription: A large door of pale wood, broad enough for impressive entrances while keeping a warmer, friendlier face than dark timber or iron. It is favored by villas, halls, and bright storefronts where welcome must still look expensive.
flavorText: “Light wood smiles; the hinges still charge full price.”

name: massive doors
shortDescription: A matched set of massive doors built for grand halls, temples, estate gates, or any owner who believes entry should require a statement. They are difficult to haul, hang, and afford, which is exactly why wealthy buyers want them.
flavorText: “When one door is not enough pride, buy two.”

name: cloth table
shortDescription: A table draped with cloth to soften rough wood, hide scratches, or create a display fit for goods that deserve better than bare planks. Market sellers love it because a clean cloth can make ordinary wares look freshly chosen.
flavorText: “A cloth table is a stall pretending to be a shop.”

name: large shelf
shortDescription: A large shelf for shops, storerooms, libraries, kitchens, and anyone who has run out of excuses for piles on the floor. It offers practical height and public order, turning scattered stock into something customers can actually browse.
flavorText: “More shelves mean fewer mysteries underfoot.”

name: rough wooden chair
shortDescription: A rough wooden chair built quickly from plain timber, with no patience for polish and no apology for splinters. It is cheap, sturdy enough, and welcome in workshops, barracks, kitchens, and taverns where elegance would only be damaged.
flavorText: “It offers a seat, not a compliment.”

name: glass beaker
shortDescription: A clear glass beaker used by apothecaries, alchemists, dyers, and careful cooks who prefer seeing trouble before smelling it. Its value depends on clean walls, sound glass, and whether the last owner rinsed away more than just color.
flavorText: “Transparent glass encourages honest mistakes.”

name: glass triangle beaker
shortDescription: A triangular glass beaker with a stable base and a shape favored for small mixtures, demonstrations, and showy shelves. It looks more scholarly than a plain cup, which helps sellers charge extra to buyers who enjoy appearing precise.
flavorText: “Some vessels hold liquid. Others hold reputation.”

name: glass flat bottle
shortDescription: A flattened glass bottle easy to pack in cases, pockets, or narrow shelf spaces. Perfumers, tincture sellers, and traveling healers like its practical shape, though careful merchants check the seams before trusting it with anything sticky or costly.
flavorText: “A flat bottle travels as if it knows space is expensive.”

name: large glass bottle
shortDescription: A large glass bottle for oils, spirits, dyes, tonics, or whatever liquid a trader wants visible enough to inspire confidence. Its size makes it useful but risky, since one careless cart wheel can turn inventory into glittering regret.
flavorText: “The larger the bottle, the louder the accident.”

name: market glass bottle
shortDescription: A common market glass bottle shaped for easy sale, repeated filling, and quick recognition on a stall. It is not rare, but clean glass with a good stopper can make simple syrup, oil, or tonic seem worth a better coin.
flavorText: “Half the cure is the bottle looking trustworthy.”

name: glass rectangle bottle
shortDescription: A rectangular glass bottle that stacks neatly in crates and sits squarely on shelves, much loved by merchants who count space as carefully as coin. Its straight sides make labels easy, even when the contents remain suspiciously vague.
flavorText: “Corners make inventory behave.”

name: glass round bottle
shortDescription: A round glass bottle with a familiar shape suited to tonics, oils, colored spirits, and decorative liquids that look better when light bends through them. It is easy to sell because buyers already know how it should feel in the hand.
flavorText: “Round bottles make even doubtful brews look traditional.”

name: glass square bottle
shortDescription: A square glass bottle designed for tidy packing, orderly shelves, and merchants who dislike wasted corners. It suits inks, acids, tinctures, and measured goods, provided the buyer remembers that sharp geometry does not make glass any less fragile.
flavorText: “A square bottle promises discipline until it hits stone.”

name: glass triangle bottle
shortDescription: A triangular glass bottle with an unusual silhouette, useful for specialty brews, rare perfumes, or vendors who want their goods recognized from across a crowded stall. Its shape catches attention, which is often the first ingredient in price.
flavorText: “Odd bottles sell ordinary liquids with suspicious ease.”

name: market glass cup
shortDescription: A simple glass cup suitable for taverns, tasting stalls, apothecary samples, and households that want drinkware clearer than horn or clay. It is fragile, but customers enjoy seeing what they are about to swallow before pretending that helps.
flavorText: “Clear cups leave fewer places for bad wine to hide.”

name: glass distiller
shortDescription: A glass distiller made for separating, purifying, and concentrating liquids under the patient eye of an alchemist. It is delicate, expensive, and often bought with great seriousness by people who will later claim the smoke was expected.
flavorText: “Distillation is the art of making a liquid explain itself.”

name: glass double flask
shortDescription: A double flask joined in clever glasswork, useful for paired reagents, mirrored samples, or experiments where two liquids must remain close but not yet acquainted. It impresses apprentices and worries masters, which is usually a fair measure of value.
flavorText: “Two chambers, one mistake waiting for permission.”

name: glass flask
shortDescription: A standard glass flask with a narrow neck, useful for storing, shaking, heating, or showing off mixtures whose colors improve their price. Apothecaries buy them constantly because every serious shelf needs vessels that look like decisions are happening.
flavorText: “A flask makes guessing look like method.”

name: glass goblet
shortDescription: A glass goblet with a lifted stem and enough elegance for feasts, tasting rooms, and nobles who prefer their drink to sparkle before it disappoints. It sells better when unchipped, clean, and kept far from clumsy servants.
flavorText: “A goblet raises wine and expectations together.”

name: glass icicle flask
shortDescription: A slender glass flask shaped like an icicle, made for cold elixirs, rare perfumes, or dramatic alchemists with a taste for fragile beauty. Its narrow form is awkward but memorable, and memorable vessels often earn unreasonable margins.
flavorText: “It looks frozen even when filled with trouble.”

name: glass jar
shortDescription: A clear glass jar for herbs, salts, insects, preserved fruit, powders, or anything a trader wants customers to inspect without touching. Its usefulness is plain, but a tight lid and clean glass can decide whether goods look fresh or forgotten.
flavorText: “A jar is trust with a lid.”

name: large glass jar
shortDescription: A large glass jar suited to bulk herbs, preserved goods, pickled roots, or display stock meant to tempt buyers by volume. It is impressive when full, accusatory when empty, and always one careless elbow away from becoming a cleanup fee.
flavorText: “Large jars make abundance visible and accidents memorable.”

name: small glass jar
shortDescription: A small glass jar for rare spices, costly powders, ointments, seeds, or samples too valuable to leave in open bowls. It fits neatly in cabinets and travel kits, making it a favorite of apothecaries who sell by pinches rather than scoops.
flavorText: “Small jars are where expensive things pretend to be harmless.”

name: glass pitcher
shortDescription: A glass pitcher for serving water, wine, cordial, or whatever a host wants displayed in generous clarity. It is elegant at table and risky in crowded inns, but good glass makes even ordinary drink look worth pouring slowly.
flavorText: “Hospitality looks cleaner when the pitcher catches light.”

name: glass retort
shortDescription: A curved glass retort used for heating, redirecting vapor, and making simple work look wonderfully dangerous. Alchemists prize the shape because it guides transformation, while merchants prize it because replacement glass is always in demand.
flavorText: “The retort bends steam and straightens excuses.”

name: complex glass triple flask
shortDescription: A complicated triple flask of connected glass chambers, built for elaborate mixtures, comparative samples, or laboratories where confidence has outrun caution. It is a costly piece, less tool than conversation, and best packed by someone with steady hands.
flavorText: “Three flasks joined together triple the science and the bill.”

name: large glass vat
shortDescription: A large glass vat for visible brewing, steeping, dyeing, or alchemical suspension, impressive enough to make customers believe the process is refined. Its transparency is useful, its bulk troublesome, and its replacement cost a quiet threat to any workshop.
flavorText: “Nothing says careful craft like a huge breakable container.”

name: market glass vial
shortDescription: A small market vial made for doses, samples, perfumes, inks, and tiny promises sold at suspiciously confident prices. It is cheap glass until filled, then suddenly becomes medicine, luxury, or evidence depending on the stall.
flavorText: “A vial is how a merchant sells one sip as a solution.”

name: simple cross gravestone
shortDescription: A simple cross gravestone carved for modest graves, roadside memorials, and families with more grief than coin. It is plain work, but honest stone carries meaning well enough when names are carefully cut and promises are quietly made.
flavorText: “Plain markers still ask the living to remember.”

name: massive gravestone
shortDescription: A massive gravestone meant for wealthy families, famous dead, or anyone whose memory is expected to occupy more space than their body. Quarrymen charge for the stone, haulers charge for the suffering, and masons charge for making grief permanent.
flavorText: “Some names are carved large enough to argue with time.”

name: detailed gravestone
shortDescription: A detailed gravestone with careful carving, decorative edges, and room for names, dates, symbols, or final boasts. It sells to families who want remembrance shaped with skill, and to estates where even mourning must observe proper taste.
flavorText: “The chisel adds what memory fears it will lose.”

name: market gravestone
shortDescription: A market-ready gravestone kept in stock for practical mourners, village yards, and buyers who cannot wait for custom carving. It is plain enough to move quickly, solid enough to matter, and priced for grief that arrives without appointment.
flavorText: “Even sorrow has urgent orders.”
